speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the Steffen family came to locate out here in this area, in the Palouse Country?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, this grandfather, he both divorced his first wife, my father, mother, in the east, you know, and he took my father and his sister and married with him.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: And he kept the rest of them, stayed with the mother, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: So anyway, he married again, and he married a woman named Moser, or in the name of Altman, and had this family, and his brother -in -law, his brother -in -law was named Nausiger, and he was a German Canadian. And he settled in homesteaded over on Union Flat here. My grandfather settled up at Farmington.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Now, I heard that a long time afterwards, not since I was a youngster, I didn't know that at all, but I used to go over and visit this Nausiger over in Pullman, because my father in the end was a good friend all the time, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I mean, after, because he was a youngster, he probably knew them and stayed around. Well, it seems like it. My father was 39 when he married, but during that time, you know, he left, he wouldn't stay with his father very long. And the girl married, she married somebody named Flarg, and lived over in Shelton, Washington, around in there. Then she had a daughter, and that daughter and granddaughter visited me here one time, and I had a address someplace, they did live in Oregon. But I only met them one time, and my sister kept corresponding with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did your grandmother move to Moscow?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, they got it clean this way. Now, my father tells me this, and I'm quite young, you know, he said he was a teamster working for the army down on the Mexican border. And John Charles Fremont Steffen, named after John Charles Fremont, the lawyer, you know, and army general, I get a lot of surveying in the west. Someone said that the man here in the army named him Steffen, any relation to him, I don't know. He didn't know he had a brother or sister, he was too young. So he met the talker, and it was Franklin, and he was the oldest one of the boys, and he was in the army, and eventually I think retired. Then that's how my father met.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, he'd been out here in the east, west, back and forth, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: And near as I know, that's the reason they come, but I don't know for sure at all. I know that the other family come out, so anyway, when they came out here, that's why Will had this little piece of property, and how it worked and so on, I don't know, but he brought his mother, because the family stayed together that way. Then Henry, he homesteaded down at a gath on this side of the Clearwater River, which was a gath, was this little railroad station right across the river. And after Will was killed, or killed himself here, he didn't get, they didn't shoot him, like all they wanted to. My grandmother, according to my mother, had the pistol under her skirt when she waved the white flag for him to quit shooting at the house.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: You know, that hearsay stuff, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, hearsay's all we got. So anyway, Henry took my grandmother to live with him down there in that homestead at Gaffa. Henry F. Now, in there, there's this girl, Luella, she's a sister. And my grandfather met her later on, I knew it, she knows, baby, he's only four or five years old. She married a man named Ferdinand Rietz, and he was a German from Germany, and he was a Spanish -American war veteran. Went to the Philippines to fight there, see, come back.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well then, they homesteaded down there right next to this Uncle Henry, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Now, I can take you right by the place, the old barn, the house still standing. I've been down there, I was a youngster, but I got four, five, six, eight years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's near Juliaetta, right? Well, they'd go out and do a letter over the road, that little pot last creek, and go up over the hill to where the Clearwater, see? And they'd go down there, like about a mile or two miles being down to the Clearwater River, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: They used to get all their wood off the Clearwater River in the spring, and by roping it and pulling it in, you know, all that logs and everything floating down the river years ago. Well, when Henry had my grandmother down there, some neighbor got stuff about a hog running loose or something, I don't know, but anyway, getting a garden or something, he was sitting at the table, you know, eating dinner at night, you know, dark.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Light was on, grandma was serving him, this guy shot through the window and killed him, see? So she had two sons killed, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: One guy, one guy. Grandmother, she saw him get killed then.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, she was right there, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: So, then this aunt, Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Reach, had her come and live with her, see? Oh, what happened to this fella who shot him?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, he got ten years. That's all he got out of it. I don't know how many years you started to know, I don't know what to name it, but I was very, very young, very, very young.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Seven or eight years old, something like that, when I got word to Moscow, my father went down and went to the trial down in Lewis and Toot, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: So on, so on, I think. Well, then Mrs. Reach took care of this grandmother, and, oh, she passed away, now you must remember she's getting up in years quite a bit.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: She didn't speak very much English, my mother couldn't understand her, you know, very well, but they didn't get along too well, as I understand, but I think my mother should have made more of an effort, too, but she was pressing German quite set in her way, see? And you got folks wrote that Will was mean to his mother, that was a crock of shit, by God, see? He was good to her, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I took care of her, and so did Henry took care of her, and then Lou Ella took care of her. Franklin, he helped, he came into Moscow, and hell, I was so little I could just merely remember him.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: He helped lay the sewers and the water lines into Moscow, he used to have them at privies and wells, you know?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: He worked on that, and he ended up living over until he died over here at Richland, Washington. And my father went and brought him over and buried him, too, and all of them were buried in the Pullman Cemetery.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: See, the grandmother and the four boys are my father's first family, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: My grandfather's first family. Sounds like your grandmother, she really saw a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She came over, she was born over there in the old country?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: She was born in Germany, too, so was my grandfather. Born in Germany.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To see two sons die.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me there was one some have to do with the drug store? Something happened?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Yeah, well, I think his name was Willis, man. He got his wife, and he'd get pretty drunk. She took a few shots at him, he'd run down the street one time. That's what my father told me. Now, I didn't see these things. That's all hearsay, anyway.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Hear -say what it is, see? Well, what did Will do for a living? My uncle?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Yeah. He was a butcher here. He had a slaughterhouse, and he butchered cattle and sold to these things. That's what the whole damn trouble started about. The building is still standing out there in the place where the walls are about two feet thick and all sawdust in them. The door is a foot and a half wide. It goes in there and stays tight. Overhead of it, the sawdust at the back one time had a platform to pull in ice. We pulled up ice and put it in the top over there, and it stayed all year round in that sawdust. That was a coal room. But the trouble was Watkins was kind of a crusader. Then we all needed them, I guess, but he was fetishizing doing something my uncle Will... Beef, you know. Hell, he took care of it as good as anybody else did. In fact, if he had the best place there was, he had a better place than Hale and Kittley ever thought he had. They had to butcher and bring right to town so they could hang up in the coal room in town. The last one of those kind of rooms that was out was the sanitary and meat market had it. It was up there on Thursday. Three buildings along there before you hit the market time. That was the sanitary and meat market. They had a coal room with the same damn thing. Sawdust walls and everything. Eventually they got fixed a little better. They tore it out, that's how it was, by getting the coal rooms. So your uncle, he could have the meat hang and he just could leave it there and stay good. It was cold enough, you know. Meat should age a little bit, you know. General rule, you know, always stop bringing meat in.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: You don't have to hang it up for two weeks to age, you know that?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: It's much better, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what was the story?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: So Watkins, you think Watkins, the story was that Watkins was mad at...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't think he was keeping his meat good?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, I don't think he was mad. I think what he did is brought it up that the meat wasn't... Well, something was wrong with it. Now that's what Dad said, you know. Cause you know that here, see, he'd come back out here. He said, that's over here, see, so I don't know anything true about it or not. But he thought that, your Dad thought that was the reason that your uncle went after him. Yeah, but I tell you, as a young man, I grew up in... Well, a man died here named Tom Kimmling. His wife Hattie is still living. The other place over on... Eltham Street there. And Hattie, Tom told me, said he knew Will. Kelly said he was a hell of a good fellow, said he had a temper, all right. Carl Smith was our first fire chief in Moscow. He had a transfer rig here, you know. Had horses, you know, first bent these trucks, you know. Did all kind of moving things, you know, and transfer work, you know, they call it. He was a fire chief here for many years. Yes, well he knew Will. Those are the only two people I knew that knew Will in town that I ever met.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he think of Will?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, he just said, no, we never talked about it too much at all. Earl of Carnwell, he's older than I am. He had the paper that you have, see. I mean, his folk can't pay for nothing when you have. But that's taken off the... One of the porters at the Adihone down here, they copied that one you got in that paper, see. But the other one, I had both copies of it in my mother. She had to destroy them, you know, it was too bad. She was just sitting there waiting about it. She didn't want anything done about it. It was too bad she had to do it, you know, but I didn't. I had them, you know, and I wanted to keep them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the way you've heard it, what happened that day, the day they both got killed?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, the way it happened, he had a horse, you know. And the watering trough is on Second Street there. Well, anyway, where he met the doctor at, I don't know, but he shot him and he slumped over his dashboard and the horse trotted on down to the office now, like I said. But anyway, he watered his horse there with a... Someone said the doctor killed him, and Will said I did it. And he rode off down to Main Street, he shot Creighton through the arm, and then his horse was shot out and then he made him on the way home. And this girl out here at Driscoll says he went up over six feet, he held it. Well, he ran a horse over six feet, you know, and I think they're easier out through that, which he did do, you know, but he lost his horse, but the fuel out there was all full of shock. It had grain, you know, there was boundage, you know, and shockage, you know, and he was standing with them hid behind that creek too, you know. There were three bridges on a half a mile there one time, you know, and you only got the cement one now, but there's two on past that, see. The creek went clear out and the field came around, and they dug it right down along side the road, see. Our place there had the creek run through it, we had... Dad had three acres in there, that was Uncle Will's hill, and he came here when he came here, he just paid the taxes on it for several years and got it called tax dates, see. But he did go to the fence, getting the steel stone for Uncle Will, plus he buried my grandmother and my uncles over there too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Will went home and then what happened after that?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, he got home, you know, and he didn't have very much examination, so he got shooting at the house all the time, you know. Shooting at it, you know, shot out the windows, I guess. I saw a bullet hole in the house, in the youngster, but the house had burnt down, but he'd run out of bullets, so he just shot himself, he knew that he was going to get him, I guess. That's the nearest I could find out about it, so I don't know for sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Any more than that?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Don't hear, see, it all did. Well, the story that I've heard is that all these guys went down, they broke open the hardware store and they all took guns out of the hardware store in there. Collins had a hardware store, and I don't know, I think everybody had a gun. Creighton got shot in the arm by a guy. You get to talk when he should have been listening.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a note that they found on him?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: They said he had a list of men that he wanted to get, that Jolly and Held were on that list as well. I heard that too, but my father, he didn't know about it, but I heard that thing, you know, they had a list of Held, but you know, after when I was a youngster, now there was Held and Kittley had witcher -tired slaughterhouse, it was just about a quarter of a mile due east of our house where we lived out there. And I don't know, anyway, my father knew how to butcher, although people in those days used to have to do it, and he used to go up and help them butcher a lot of these people, you know, take hogs, you know, as well as knock the beef down, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He worked with Held and Kittley?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, you know, he wouldn't work a study, every once in a while they wouldn't know he wanted to work. But at that time, you know, you didn't save all the things you do today, you'd save everything on a pig with a squeal, you know, the only thing to throw away on a cow, you know, is a tail bag, that's the only the hard part. But I ate a lot of heart and liver in those days, so it was free, see. We didn't have a place big enough there, we had a cow, and eventually later on we had a pig or two, you know, we'd feed, you know, up and butcher. But my mother, the heads of hogs, you know, they threw them away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But those days, you know, the wars, a woman had to call a boiler, you ever hear of them, what they are?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: And put it on the stove, you know, and boil the stove, boil the water out, boil the soap in, and boil the dirt out, that's what they used. Anyway, she put a couple of these hog heads in there, boiled them for a day, and they cleaned them up, my father helped her and grind that up and made head cheese. Now that's real head cheese, old Teddy, doesn't like that crap you buy in the store now.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: We ate a lot of head cheese in those days, now we had a pan that was probably about 10 or 12 inches across diameter, maybe two inches high, and that put it in there, and then it was settled in all real good, and then they cut it out in chunks to eat, you know, like that, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: But we didn't, we had a pantry in the house, but we had that there place that my uncle kept cool, and we put ice in it, we could put things in there, and we'd keep fruit, we'd keep vegetables, we'd keep everything in there all winter long, and there it would freeze and keep not, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father and uncle learn how to butcher before they came out here? Were they trained to be butchers?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: No, no, I don't think they were, but the whole thing is that everybody in those days, well, all the farmers, you know, they'd get together and they'd call a butcher and bee, and butcher a bunch of hogs, you know, they had a tub and had the fire on it, and the water boiling, and they'd dip the old hog in there and pull her out on board, and scrape her, you know, scrape the hair off of it, you know, but they had a slide and so on, and everybody got doing things like that, and you hung them up, you know, and cut them open, and got them doing things like that, and those times, you either went down and salt pork, or if you had to smoke, a lot of times you'd use that there, you'd put all different things on it, salt them to beat hell, you know, and smoke them, see, and eat it, you know, you didn't have that type of way of taking care of your meat like you got today, so some of them used to can some meat too, you know, that was good, you know, that was real quick, but mostly all the hog meat I ever had when I was a kid was salted, went down in a big wooden barrel, salted, see, we made a barrel of sauerkraut every fall too, you know, that was just cutting on, put the salt on it, you know, and goes down for a minute, had a board over the top, it wasn't a rock on it to hold it down in there, that sauerkraut was the best medicine a kid ever took.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How's that, did it help your ills?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh, I tell you, you drink a glass of sauerkraut in fifteen minutes, you take a crap, it just runs right through you practically, you know. Cleaned you out then. Oh, I'll say it would clean you out. Yeah, sauerkraut, you used to be able to buy it in the stores in the can, you know, sauerkraut juice, but now I don't think they make, I haven't put it out anymore, but it was good. We used to drive clear over to the Germans over there at Uniontown and bring back a whole, two or three sacks of cabbage bag, and then they had a board that cut it, you know, and slack it off, and work it back, you know, you wouldn't touch your hand, they'd sit in there and push it back and forth over these cutters, you know, that way it goes down the barrel and so on. Well, that's why you had to do your things like that to have anything to eat, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's, then, your family did it because it was old German custom to have sauerkraut, isn't it?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh, yeah, but then a lot of people had it too, I get it. That was a damn good thing to have, sauerkraut.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What other parts of the pig did you use too?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I figure you must have used most of it, one way or another. Well, you take the jowls of a hog head, you know, you cut that off and have a jowl of bacon, they call it, to cure it, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's bacon?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Yeah, like, you could cure that too, but take the whole thing and, you know, and boil it off, then cut it out and then everything, you know, goes into making this head cheese. Now, of course, I wouldn't have no idea, I was young in those days. Oh, yeah. I was the oldest at last. The memory of doing it out there is probably about 12 years old. Yeah. That's a good eating, because we raised quite a garden, and we used various things in the ground. You ever see how the pit vegetables and potatoes, you dig a hole in the ground, maybe about a foot and a half, two foot deep, and you put a lot of straw in it, and you put some burlap in it, or any old cloth you put on, put your potatoes in there and different vegetables, and you'd cover it over with a cloth, and you'd cover it over with lots of straw, and then you put the dirt over it about a foot and a half thick.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: So it can't freeze, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: And when you wanted to get anything, you opened a little hole in it, and got in there, and my dad pushed me in there to pull out stuff. He didn't want it too big. Many times, he'd pull out them potatoes, and carrots, and like that, and then he'd close it up good, put the dirt back on it.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: If it was too frozen, why, go get a real barrel full of manure, put it against it, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: That was top it up, but there was no air, get in and freeze anything. Oh, we are.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Did that work better than a root cellar? Did that keep better? It works about the same thing, see, but that's why you didn't have any expenses, you got to get it on your labor, see? To pit it, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: You can do it for an easier root cellar.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: It's just like a cellar on the side of a hill, you know, and you got it boarded up and everything else, and it keeps things from freezing, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh, it's ordinary, it's like that building out there we had.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: They didn't freeze in that building either, Mother had her fruit in it, you know, and so on like that, but you didn't have too much room, so the potatoes were buried in the pitlock, see? Did you get a lot at one time? Did you get a whole bunch of stuff at once? You'd have a bunch, we didn't have to go back very often?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh yeah, we'd get out, you know, and after the last two or three weeks probably, you know, they got quite a bit, but hell, you'd probably be buried about 10 or 12 sacks of potatoes in there. That's real interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you garden much in those days?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, we had to raise everything.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: You didn't come to town and buy anything in the grocery store, you didn't have anything like that, but we made butter, you take it, put it in your head, your own thing to put it in one pound or two pound thing, and you had your own wrapper on it, and you brought it in the grocery store and they put it in there, and people come in and buy, no idea what day you're going to bring it, they'd buy that butter, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: They didn't even have it in the fridge region for it, but you had it wrapped up good in a, in a kind of a silken -like paper -like, you know, like that. But you could buy some lemons and some oranges, you know, and you could buy potatoes, and a few things like that, and see, in the grocery store, but sugar, you know, and all those kind of things, but you didn't have these type of vegetables in those days you got now. You didn't have them in season, you know, yeah, you might find some in season.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did your father, could he make a living pretty much doing this stuff? Was that how he got fired? Did he have to work out too?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, he was a teamster, he had a teamster, but he was sectioned to that cemetery out there in Moscow for two or three years one time. He was, got $60 a month, got paid in three, $20 gold pieces, I remember seeing them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Twenty?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Yeah, but we had that little acreage, didn't pay nothing, only taxes, only $10 or $12 a year. We raised potatoes, raised gardens, a lot of gardens, had a fruit tree on it, had the team that did work with the team, you know. We had enough, sent some hay down there, had some hay for them, you know, and he raised a coke every year, maybe two coke, and sold them. And those things happened, you know. You don't take so much to make a living and things like that. He didn't want very much, didn't have, I remember when we, the first telephone we got cost a dollar a month for it, put in, you know. I remember the first phonograph had no Edison to give to it, horn on it, circular, circular disc. You know, which he didn't have, he didn't have to take or keep it, you know, they were something today, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the first car?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, we didn't have cars, you know, I had ponies up till I was in my 20s, I didn't have a car. I had some damn good ponies to ride, until of course I had a car for it, I went in the service too, but, the first car I had was a Ford T -model, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you used to get where you wanted to go on ponies then?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, we rode the trains, you know, it was cold candy, go to call backs, pull them when they rode the train all the time. Oh yeah, didn't cost only 25 cents, 30 cents, ready for a pony on the train. You know, we had a little bug, they called it one car, drive it up from here to call back, see, went down twice a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the service was good?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh yes, we had an inland, that was the Great Northern, but it was an electric train from Moscow to Spokane. The depot still stands down there on A Street, you know, there's all the electric coming through there, and all the freight come in, and the cars weren't electric, but the motors, you know, brought them in, all the electricity. That was built in the 1900s, early, that's when Mrs. Brecker would tell you about the whorehouse being down there on A Street, where, you know, that Hodgin went to. That was built with teams, they didn't have no tractors or anything back in those days, they were all teams by the time, the roadwork was all done by plows, breaking plows, and teams and scrapers. Some was four horses, some was two horses, they used on us, see. That's how it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They take that, you take from, you go out in that, it's called White Avenue now, you know White Avenue?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Yeah. Well, when you go up to the corner, and that creek comes out and comes down along that road for a quarter of a mile, see, now there used to be a bridge coming out of that place, and went on across from Scatterville Field, and then come back again, so they took those two bridges out and cut right down along that road. And they used horse and teams back then, and dug the dirt out of it, put it in the middle of the road, and scattered it around, and stuff like that. Anyway, band name is Stillinger, we're Rose Boss, and they gave me fifty cents a day to carry water to these men. And I had these water bags stuck along, and I'd go to the farmhouse at our place and fill them up and keep them going because it was hot in the summer months, and I drank quite a little water, and I got fifty cents a day. And they worked twelve days on that, and I got six bucks. And it came to me as a warrant from the county, see. My mother brought me to town to get some cool clothes, and the penny store was then called the Golden Rule. And she got me a pair of shoes, and the stockings were rib stockings, and they come up above the knee. I got two pairs of those, and I got a blue Oxford suit with a belt across the back of it, and two pairs of pants for that six dollars. I had to think about it. She got all that for me for that six dollars. It didn't sound like much when you said six bucks, now it sounds like a little more. Yeah, a pair of shoes, two pairs of stockings, and this suit. Well that suit I think was about $3 .95, something like that. It was two pairs of trousers. I wore it all school year. I just progressed, you know, and then I had an old pair of pants to put on otherwise. The shoes got on by the dark.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was this work they were doing with moving the dirt and all that?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: They dug the creek. They were digging a new bed there. They dug the creek, and you see it right now, it comes right alongside the road, see. And they bought the land, I think they bought the land off of the white addition there. I think it came off of the white. It might have, well, I don't think it came off the Scatterville property. It might have come off the Scatterville property, I don't know. But you drive the road there now, and you see how the creek, across the cement bridge, see. Now there was two bridges between that and Mountain View Road, see, there was two roads. Then you turn and go north on Mountain View Road, there's another wooden bridge there. Now they've got a culvert in there, you know, a big culvert, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you just a boy then?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: You were probably just... I was born there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I mean, boy, you were just a kid when you were carrying water, right?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I was 10 or 12 years old, 10 or 11 years old, something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they had many men doing that job?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh yeah, man, my father worked on it, I think. They got two horses on a scraper, and sometimes they used four horses on a scraper. And they had to go along and plow this, you know, they plowed, so that they would be loose enough to pick up, and they'd bring it up, you know, and so on. And they worked it in for the fence in afterwards, and so on. But I think at 12 days, I think it worked out, because I know I got a truck for, a warrant from the county for $12.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the way you heard that whorehouse story?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, I heard a few stories, you could grow up and so forth, but the only one I heard was Mrs. Baker telling about it. She said that, Ha -ha, Hodgins went there and held Mrs. Baker, and I don't know, I mean, Mrs. Hodgins, I don't know anybody else, went and bribed him, and he had to take the place of it when I was in. He stuck with his own wife, and she stuck his watch. Said the next day, he went and said, this morning, he always turned the plate upside down, you know, and he turned his plate over for breakfast, and there were ladies watching. And that's what Mrs. Baker told me, see. But the Shannon sisters had a village that my father used to talk about, and I was awfully young, they went around, heard the Shannon sisters, was raised or something like that, and they lived on that. It would be the university property off of Sweet Avenue, just off of Main Street down there. But the last little whorehouse around the country was a pioneer over in Colfax. There had to have been a zit. Hell, I've been into that several times. I got big enough to go, I mean, I was 16, 17, 18, you know, something like that. There had to have been it over there. Of course, Lewiston had it. Fort Nuff is up and down Main Street, and over on the river, up until World War II. And when they brought the soldiers and the sailors here to go to school for training for six weeks, something like that, the university had their, you know, they didn't have those co -ops. Like Willis Sweet Hall had the Navy in it, and Hayes Hall and Forney Hall had their army up there. And then they all had some army in that too, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they do? They went down to Lewiston?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Then they, well, had weekends, you know, they let them off Saturday afternoon, and they go down to a whorehouse and they got to have a little money. Of course, you know, the goody -goody people, they couldn't pack that. They're just like you got in the paper here, there's a paper in my paper about this abortion law. These guys are crying, they're goddamn guys out there, and these people, you know, got a little religion about abortion. I don't know what church they think about it, but I lived in different countries there, and I think England, they got a, for an abortion law, but you should do it for three months, and Japan does it all the time. Too much population. But, and here they're hollering their heads off now. I don't think it hurts anything after three months at all.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I don't think it should take something alive, because I know my, my daughter -in -law has two, what do you call it, baby born, you know, by cutting her open, how do you call that, you know?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: The Caesarean. The Caesarean, yeah, two, Caesarean. And both of them had taken at least 30 days before their time, and so, you know, they take them, see, have the good healthy babies, you know, like this, see. I don't believe in taking a child on an abortion, when you know it's six months along, something like that, because a lot of children live with six or seven months, you know. And of course, a lot of girls, you know, have babies. Six or seven months after they get married, you know. I don't mean that guy that conceived, you know, as like, and I used to say, they come a little early, you know. I remember one time, that woman that I got, she was knocked up, she was going to have a baby, I mean, her little girl was about four years old. She said, we're going to have a baby sister for Christmas. And her mother says, what are we going to do, I'll be sore -footed. Ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha. Well, you see, it wasn't going to come quite that soon. Ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha. Oh, yeah. I hope you ain't got that thing working. I have, yeah, it doesn't matter though, they're good stories. Ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha. The language ain't so good. Ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha ha. Oh, language is fine, I'll tell you. A lot of guys, that's the way people talk. You know, it's all salty. Well, I did it quite a bit anyway. That's a good story. I put in some time, I didn't retire, I was in the service for a nine year Marine Corps. I traveled quite a little bit too, and then my mother was alone, so I took a special order and discharged, I asked them to discharge me, came back to visit me, and I got married. I had three boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you go into Marines?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: 1924. I got my final discharge in 1941. If I had reserves, I'm in between the two of these. I had active duty plus reserve. I got five discharges out of the Marine Corps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you serve there in the early years, in the 20s?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, in the 20s, in the late 20s, I went aboard a ship. In 1925, I went to Australia and New Zealand, and the islands and so forth, and then came back, and then Maryland, California, and I was over in Brownsham quite a little while, and then I was out of the service for a while, and I went back in that time, I went to the islands, and then to China. I was in Shanghai for four years, or just about, not quite, in there. Just last night, I was showing Bill here. You know you save things on, well, save some things in China too, but the first time I board that ship, you know, we had a little weekly paper to put out telling about things. I got copies of the same three years, 1925, 22nd party, 1927, just two pages telling all about things we did, athletics, going to all those trips and so on like that, as well as that magazine, and all those things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any exciting adventure, anything happen to you in those early years that you remember? Did you have any close calls in foreign countries or anything?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: No, not, not, nothing. It was all peace time that way, the only thing that bothered about it was that our government was so chintzy about doing anything that they wouldn't spend any money. I got $21 a month in the horse blankets when I first went in as a privacy in the service, and when I got discharged, I was a sergeant, and I was only getting $54. Of course, I got 10 % longevity on top of that, plus rifle qualifications. Now that was the pay, and that was up to 1941.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Rifle qualifications?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, the pay, you know, you've got to be a sharpshooter or an expert, you know, like that. I used to shoot pretty damn good, I thought, when we had those old Winchesters, you know, 30 -06.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that is, now, you said that maybe he wasn't really a doctor?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, he was a doctor, just saying that when somebody was a lawyer, and still they didn't go to the law school. I think he studied with a doctor, what all the information I ever heard after it. Maybe it was 10 years he studied and learned all that doctor's doing.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Nowadays, you'd have to go to a medical school, wouldn't you?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Now, take for instance, Rogers, either Roger St. John's father. He was one of the best criminal lawyers there ever was in Los Angeles. But he studied with a lawyer down there. He never went to a law school, see. And you take Harvey Smith, he was our city engineer here for years and years. But his father, by any chance, was an attorney, and he studied with a lawyer. He did all things like that, but had his thing while I was an attorney, but he wasn't right away from any law school or anything like that. Sort of an apprentice, like that. He learned to do a lot of things, you know, he would make deeds, you know, and do a lot of things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's this business about the sewage? Was Watkins against having the good sewage put in town?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh, no, no, no, no, no, no, no. He was a crusader. He wanted to get rid of all the privilege in the wells, and wanted to get it all in like that. And it was a good thing, I think, too. I know my uncle Franklin, after putting in some, I tell you, I helped work for the city one time in the late 30s, 39s, on South Washington Street. We dug up some old sewer water lines in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what they're made of?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Wood. There was wood with wire around them. Yeah, there was four inches. That's when they first put it in. They didn't have this. There's tile or steel, or pipes that they had today.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: They didn't have them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They wouldn't, was they? Oh, was your, this other thing here, was Will the first one in the family to be buried over there in Pullman?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Yes. My grandmother buried him over there, Helen. And Helen got the deed to it, and I had the deed. It was a cemetery life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, then your father went and got a good stone?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Afterward, my father put a stone up there, yes, to the will. And it's still there. But the rest of us have never gotten that money.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: They didn't put stones up on the place, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: But we have a lot. Of course, the city takes care of that, you know, with the city thing, you know, the mow the grass and things like that. Just one of those things. The next person to be buried there was Henry that got killed, the Will version of Henry. Then my grandmother Helen was buried there. And after, where's my, when my friend Franklin died, there was a rich man, my father brought him over and buried him there. Then my father died, I buried him there. My father died in 1937.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think that there were ever hard feelings about it that you knew about for what had happened for the family?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I never had anybody ever mention it to me in life. When I was a kid going to school or anything about that. I never had anybody mention or worry about it. And the first time it ever came out in the local favor, when Earl Cornwell gave some, put it down here, that Spokane paper he had. And it came out, now let's see, it was in August, about 51 or 52, something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did I say, 51?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I think so, about 51 there.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, you see, my second boy was born on August the 4th, 1948, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: And I think he was four years old. He'll be about 52. So anyway, old Bill Marinol had that. I went down and chewed his ass out.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I said, you're on the yellow sheet, are you, God damn you?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: You know, I knew him, it was World War I, you're running the yellow sheet, aren't you, down there. Well, no, no, I said, the hell you ain't.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I said, you had to go back a hell of a long way to give me a bad time, didn't you?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Because it didn't hurt me. I didn't know anybody knew anything about it without chewed his damn old ass out anyway. The other time, I thought, you don't see that much in the paper. Somebody said something about digging up something happened way back years ago. Oh, you know, my God, you know. What reason you had to die is no more, you know, much as I do. God said, give me a little bad time, you know. I guess you had a temper. Well, I guess, you know, usually every town's got a couple of early murders, and they trot them out to tell you this is what it was like in the old days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, there wasn't much law, right?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: They say there wasn't much law. I don't know whether the sheriff had a W. I think he had one. There was a one -day man, one -night man on the police force in Moscow when I was a kid growing up. That's all they had. They had a one -night police officer and one -day police officer. And you take Grant Robbins, who was a police officer here in the daytime for a year, and then Stanley, he was a night officer. He used to go in the past, where that's at, your aunt Rose, had a place in there. And he had his gun hung up in the back of the kitchen, I guess, and he wanted to go out to it, and he'd go put it on and go out, and look around. And walk around. But you didn't have the traffic, you had a horse, buggy, but eventually you got to have a few little cars, but you didn't have that thing going on like you got today.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean there wasn't so much crime because it was harder to get around?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Harder to get around. Well, I don't know we had a little thing to do. We used to have sleigh rides, and you know, like that. I drove a team, that team had a cutter or sled, and they'd go out in the evening, drive around sometimes, and get a big sled and hay rack full of hay, and get all the kids off in the church, you know. Sunday school, I got young people, you know, put a blanket over here, and sat there, and put his finger behind his back, and take a ride in the hay. Yeah, you know, go out for two or three hours, have four horses, maybe trot along, you know, have quite a time, bring a foot warmer along with you, if you want to do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With the sleigh ride?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Yeah, but he had a lot of fun. He had a lot of fun in those days. You'd have little meat houses, and have dances around there, so maybe they'd have a place where they'd take up the furniture out and have a dance, you know. You know, they'd have a little fun, have a country out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the dances go on for a long time? Did they go late into the night?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: No, they started at eight o 'clock, and people got their droves, were coming in the car. I went down a lot, and I was younger, and they broke up about one o 'clock, and that kind of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Music? They had music, a lot of music?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, they had a, one woman had an organ, and the other guy had a violin, maybe they had a Jew's heart, and that was about the size of a harp, you know. They didn't have very much, two pieces, about all, you know. And you'd dance a circle two -step, or a two -step, or you'd have a waltz, and that was about all you had, see. Course, you didn't have the giddy bug stuff you got today, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know all the kids that were there? They all knew each other?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Yeah, most of the time they knew everybody. What else did you do, you were just fooling around, playing, when you were a kid, having a good time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of play did you have?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: You got up at five o 'clock in the morning, and you didn't go to stay up too damn late. You didn't have the radio, eventually you got a radio, that was one of the radios right there, and that thing there had a radio in there, but it had a battery in it, six volt battery, and it was off and run down, and take it up to the pound, and it was charged up again, and eventually the thing, I took the thing out, made a little catch, and all that, put some stuff in, and that was the radio, back in the days, when it first came out, see. Stuart Warner radio, that radio thing, the cabinet cost $125. Oh, that's pretty expensive. Well, I know, but the radio was pretty high. You pick them old Vic Trollers used to wind up, and they gave them $100, $150, $200 too, back in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about, did you ever hear about how Marshall Hayes got shot there at Troy?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: That's a famous one. My father -in -law, you know, he lived out there between Troy, born out there between Troy, and you heard about the mushrooms in there, but anyway, he had a theater, see, my father -in -law had that theater in Troy one time. So the roads got good, and then people come to the mosque, get better pictures, or whatever, whatever, how to score with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think that he got it here, I was kind of abusing his wife quite a bit, you know, Sly wasn't there any name? Jake? What was his name?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Pains.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pains, huh? Well, Pains Sly, wasn't he?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Marshall, I forgot his name, Marshall Hayes. Well, anyway, he's outside, I guess he's outside the city limits, so he'd come up there and do something, and I guess, told him to stay away, none of his damn business, and he got close, he had the door open, they opened the gun there, and shot him. I guess he got some time out of it during that time, his wife divorced him, and married again, I think. But old Jake Sly, he used to be out there, he'd come to town, I remember Jake, he had a daughter, married a woman named Smith here.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: This is World War I veteran now, I know even well, we've worked with him yet, he'd been in that Robson grocery store for quite a while, sold out in the collision, and still down there, yeah? Married Selma Sly, and there's a girl, another girl, and she was married two or three times, okay?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: She was a bit of both of anybody, and there's a boy or two in the family. He got a kick out of old Jake, he got religion, he'd leave here, he'd be around 80.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He did?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh yeah, before that time he didn't have any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do when he got religion? He'd go around and tell you about it?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh, not so much that, but he had the preacher at the house most of the time, you know, some of these off -brand religions. Then after he passed away, his widow got married again to another old man, you know, and I don't know, I think he passed away, and she ended up in the late old county. I said, oh my, he passed away. I said, you have to excuse me, and then I took a little trip to the little boy's room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He came back out after your uncle died, and did that have anything to do with why he came back?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, I tell you, I have no more idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He'd been out here already before that?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: He'd been out a couple of times, I think, maybe more. He traveled around here, and there was 39, and my mother was 24, they're 15 years different than their age. Back in those days, they didn't think much about it, I don't know. Eventually my parents, they separated and forced later on to, and I thought I can't go even. My father was 77 when he died, my mother was 81 when he passed away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Listen, did you know Pat Malone at all?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: No, I knew Moody quite well, but I didn't know Pat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me a story or two about it, didn't you? About old Pat?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, all I know is that my father -in -law told me this story because he was around in that country. He said that they had these girls in the houses there in Clarkia, you see. They didn't have them in Bovill, they had them over in Clarkia. So old Pat would make a trip over there, he was about 15 miles or so, and they'd make a train run over there. But any one of these girls got in the fight with them, or lobbied over there, and he'd get a little beating on her, or cut her, or something like that. So the first train that closed the place was to bring her to Bovill to take the train out. She had to lay down on the platform, and hear from the stretcher saying, old Pat was there, and said, ah, Pat says many times, you've been over in my house and been with me. Ah, Pat says the four girls all the way back there, says I'll remember mine, I'll remember mine. Hahahaha! Hahahaha! I'll remember mine, I'll remember mine. Haha!

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about Pat and the moonshiners out there?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, you know, we see my father -in -law, and the janitor at the courthouse for quite a long summer here, up there, and Moody was a shirt, and they'd gone after a bootlegger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, I was asked before I come back here, and anyway, that old Pat that was going to get this guy, and I don't know, the guy who ran what happened, he had to get some shooting, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: And shot Moody's heel off, hahaha, I had him in the heel, that's what my father -in -law would tell me. But then they had Claus Peterson, Claus Peterson was another family member, and he had, well, as far as that goes, Agnes and Bob Peterson, an attorney, was a son, you know, Agnes, and then one of them, you know, Ellen, Agnes was driving the car over a Pullman when they went over a bank, and Ellen was killed, you know. She was a school teacher, so she retired, and oh yes, I went to school during even Jesus Christ, see, a long time ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Claus?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: He was a father, and he was a widower, and the old gentleman liked to play around a little time. Anyway, he had some moonshine he bought, see, and he had a head -sun, and somebody stole his hat.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: So he comes in there, surfing, and he's playing, but he didn't want to say he had the bootleg with him, and he says, I hate a thief, I hate a thief, he says, I hate a thief, he says, what?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I hate a thief. And then they tell another story on him, where the parking lot is now, where the Christian church the city bought and tore down there right across from their market time on 3rd street there, was a vacant place in there. So they'd have a viral meeting once a year, you know, they'd have one of those things, some Roman preacher comes here and says everybody's sinned for 30 days, and then they forget about it for another week. Anyway, they put up a big tent there, see, and somebody went out and had these little sawmills and brought in a lot of star dust, and among the star dust, you know, cutting some slivers, you know, shavings, you know, slivers and so on, they put it on the ground so it's damp, you know. Then they had some wooden benches made, so they got old Claus to go on, but he had an awful habit about every third word, Jesus Christ, I got a damn or something, you know, doing a little swearing, you know, and so they asked the preacher how to break himself into habits. So then he said, well, he said, you carry some silver dollars in your pocket, and every time you say a word you think about a hand, the nearest person will give you a dollar, and you'll break yourself into habits for a long time. Well, old Claus said he tried. So he went to that meeting that night, and of course, a couple times he was doing a little praying, and he knelt down, you know, and by the way, Gary said, when those slivers stuck him in his knees, he said, God damn son of a bitch, you know, and he just thought, then he pulled the dollar out of his pocket hand, so the next person would have to be a woman with a widow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And she took it and leaned over it and said to him, where should we go, Claus?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: So the story is... That's a good story.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So this Claus was a character, eh? You know what the tuna building is on Main Street? You know what the tuna building is?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, part of the tuna building one time was a secondhand store. It was a secondhand store, and it had the windows for different things to sell, but it had a little curtain across the seat to go back in the store.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: So the owner of the shop said, don't sell him anymore, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: So he'd get, Philop to get him a pint, you know, and he'd come into the store, and he got pretty well teed up one day, and he thought he was going to the bathroom, so he pulled that curtain loose to that little window behind him, and stood there, and you're in the heat right out in the window. Hahahaha!

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it right out of the street?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: No, no, no, you're standing right in the window, show window, hahaha! Old Philop kicked him out and let him come in anymore.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I'm going to tell you a lot of things happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear a story?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Yeah, pretty witty mind you, then. Pretty witty, alright. All the things you used to tell around, you know, because you know, you make up stories, you know, as far as that goes. And my father -in -law, you know, being in the Sheriff's office up there, and had, you know, a round editor and everything, and then old timer like his, how to try, and so on. Well, I hate to tell you about quite a few things that happened, but he died down in Twin Falls, he died in a lab of hot springs. I'll take it all back, he lived in a lot of hot springs, I think he took him to the big hospital there in Salt Lake City. He was married in Twin Falls, but the mother -in -law lives in, I think it's Sody Springs, where she lives at with her youngest daughter.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: There are a lot of stories that come out of there, just jokes, you know, jokes, you know, like that. You know, a joke is, there's only so many jokes and then they're dressed up, you know. But that's okay, that's like, their stories are told all over, you know, they go from one place to another. Oh yeah, from against the arm, right. It would fit most anything, you know. Ask Lola Clyde about it, see, Clifford Nott enlarged that picture of the school, gave her the postcard with him on. And of course you know where the Rush School, that wasn't the Rush School, so after they tore down the old building there, there was Irving School there. That was, it stood on that property and there was poplar trees all around it, you don't see the building too well in this picture. But then below it, the playground where the Rush School is now, was where the Rush School was and it burnt. Then the Whitmore School was where the annex was, that was called, I don't know if it was called Whitmore School, but I went to school, my first grade teacher was Miss Whitmore, that's why they called it the Whitmore School.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah, you remember her?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh yes, she's an awful nice teacher. Miss Whitmore, Whitworth, see, but Whitmore, her father lived to be 105 years old, lived right over here on Martin Street and she taught school and that's where that Lena Whitmore School was named after her, see, all the time teachers there were here. Where the old high school is there, that was built after that there, Rush School burnt, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know how that school burnt, you ever hear about that?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh, I always had to, I would, you know, have a big old wood burnish in there and I suppose something happened. Well then, where Pearl Cornwell lives at the 730 East and 8th Street was the school and I went to school up there, after I left that old Whitmore School, down there, Whitworth School, by the way. They had the first, second and third grade downstairs from the high school was upstairs in it, see, and they had the basement on each side, one side for the boy and one side for the girl, see. I used to go down both sides, I didn't know which side to go in, I was only five years old and went to school, I wasn't quite six, I mean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was school like when you were just in grade school, what was it like?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: We had longer hours than you got now, and you had one teacher for the whole thing to start with and eventually you got up where you had teachers for different classes, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And even here in Moscow, was one teacher for everyone?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: When I first started out, up until about the fifth grade or sixth grade, something like sixth grade, I had a teacher for just one class.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No kidding?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Yeah, maybe 30, 40 kids in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they'd be all grades in one teacher?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: No, the teacher would teach all subjects. Oh, all subjects you did. Okay, but they had different teachers for each grade. Country school, one teacher would teach up to the eighth grade, then you had to take examination in town, up at the University of Warsaw. One teacher, now take Molly Carlson, she was my fourth grade teacher, and she had a little hover hose there, and she was like, you better pay attention. She had false teeth and minor size, her teeth were quick, you know. But she was a good teacher, I don't mean maybe, but you didn't fool around with her, you were scared, you studied, see. I learned something in that grade, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it she had, what kind of hose?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh, a little rubber hose, I'll give you a few cracks with it real good, I'll tell you what she said. Up there at that place on 730 East 8th Street, they bought that land up there, and on down both sides it was prune trees. It was way, way, way back it goes in there, I don't know, about 300 feet then, all we had in school. Well, right around the cemetery there, I think this here, I'll tell you his name lives in the place, the fellow named Jensen lived there, didn't have any children, so that was home for some children. They brought two boys to live, see, and they walked to school, instead of coming on that road around, they went around and walked down the railroad track. Well, they go up to school, after that's where we went, see, now we had the same thing down the basin, each side went for the boys, one for the girls, but they had a fountain in the hallway, and they had it just running about an inch or two above, you know, in front of them studying, kids would drink. Well, the kid got to fooling around, he turned the valve down below in that basin, the boys were really, the guys that shot the water clear up the ceiling, you know, practically, see, and of course the feet would run out, and she'd run down and shut it off. Well, that was all right, but of course, she knew who did it. So she sent some kid out to get her some switches, now that day, by God, they just beat the hell out of you, and this kid cut some switches off of the prune trees and got them bigger around my thumb, you know, and she took that kid up in front of the class and beat him on the legs and back until he had welts. By God, you know, it was a shame that this pair of people were Adventists, and they let the kids suffer like that. Eric, we all felt so sorry for him, but he was a tough little bastard, by God, he didn't cry.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: But he had welts on him, by God, can you imagine that?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: By God, just, oh hell, you figure on the fourth, you know, about the third or fourth grade it was, about the fourth grade it was, maybe third grade, maybe it was the third grade. But, you know, under ten, you know, I'd say, you know, I'd get beat by a teacher should have had her damn ears knocked down in Trinity, you know, but nowadays teachers don't dare lay a hand on you, you know. What I've seen in the school, some kids those days, they'd miss school, maybe couldn't go every year, I'd go and go two or three months to work, you know, and they were pretty big and they'd give the teachers a bad time, you know, and those fellow named Randall, the principal of the school, one time, he knocked one kid down with a top off a desk that was loose by God, I was going to attack him, see, and he didn't get nothing out of that, well, maybe he had a damn good reason, but he was a big kid, you know, kid about 14 or 15 years old, probably in about the sixth grade, you know, trying to learn something, it was winter, might be three months, you know, those things happened a long time ago.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I had an aunt, she died of cancer here several years ago, but she married a man named Wilcox, they were fluke, she taught out at Coles, taught out at Randall Flats, now Randall Flats, you know, is north of Troy, you know, that way you go out by Roberson Lake, she taught in there, she taught on the school down here at Al Juliaetta, right on top of the hill, climbed that grade going up out of Juliaetta before he dropped down to the Clearwater River, she taught school there, I know, and I remember, so that's where my grandparents moved out here from Kansas, and she used to come and stay at our house in vacation time, see, I was just a kid, you know, but, you know, one time, we liked her, so well, you know, we used to, you know, hang around in Pester, you know, something like that, and one day she said to me, she said, Kenneth, you think, if you don't quit, Bob, and he said, I'm going to cut my spinner and go right up through that city, and I just thought, my gosh, she was going to do it, you know, I said, just things like that, you know, and she said, I'm going to cut my spinner, and she said, you're going to cut my spinner? Cut my spinner and go right up through the city, at that time, men didn't wear belts or anything, you know, but they wore a pair of spenders, you know, and sometimes they had them overall, mostly just, they didn't have bib -o, or something like that, they wore a pair of spenders, you know, my father wore a spender, and they had rubber in them, you know, that stretched a little bit, that's what she said, I'm going to cut my spender and go right up through the city, you know, she was quite an answer, she had two children, a boy and a girl, the boys, farms around over there, I had a poof, I was hoping to get 400 acres in, there he is, and the gutter, she married a man that spent years and years westing us people, she's living back in New York State someplace, I don't know if the girl does, she's a graduate, home economics from WSU, and Stanley's a graduate from WSU too, they're first cousins, you know, look at that rain down, it was snowing like the Dickens were this morning, was it snowing in here?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Yeah, just enough to see if that mountain, we don't have too much, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you first drink moonshine?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well, you see, it went dry in 18th, and I was 16, so I wasn't drinking it then, but we didn't have it come back to 33, you know, between that time, you know, I had a little moonshine around, you know, and well, I drank a little bit here in Moscow, then I was down in Oregon for a while, and we made a little bit out there, another place I was working at, we put our 50 gallon drum in a manure pile, and covered it up with our mash in there, and then we fermented it, because the heat from the manure pile went in a bunch of, you know, fermented, then we had a crock, a jug, that holds the top on it just like a cork in it, and the cork went in there, and the mash was put in, you know, down in there, and then it got hot, you know, we put it on the stove, then the steam came out and went through the car, and water, and dropped it out into another jug, and that way you get a little alcohol out of it that way, you see, you didn't have to mix it up, you didn't have to mix it about twice as water, it would burn your guts up, you know, that was one way to make a little moonshine, for a Saturday night dance, if you wanted to go to the drug store and buy a little bourbon flavor, you could buy it those days, and pour in it, you know, color it up a little bit, because otherwise it would be white, you know. That's what I used to call white lightning, you know, a mucal, you know, white lightning, that's what it was, the first comes off of that stuff, you know, you can do that two or three times, about 200 % in alcohol, then you can run it off, two or three times, put some more water in that mash, and you take and dilute it, the first car down, to get it down to, you know, about 100%, now you buy the whiskey, you get this, how to get anything over 90 % proof, you know, 86 % of the roof. So it really had a kick in it then. Oh, damn right it had a kick in it, it was made good, you know, but most people didn't have sense enough to take and dilute that like, I used some kind of a, something you know, like a, now you take and have a mix drink with it, you'd have 7 -up in it, or something like that, everybody tried to drink it straight, somebody would take a little water chaser out, a young kid starting out and didn't have brains enough, but he got to know what to do about it. And I went to China, we named the same thing, you couldn't buy any soft drinks out in there, you'd buy, it had Coke, you know, like that, or Pepsi Cola or something like that, similar to that, but they had a Russian stuff called Vishnik, and that would be flavored with, oh, cherries, raspberries, or something like that, see a flavor, you know, just like you used to buy, you know, years ago, and pop, you know, kind of a flavored color, you know, you don't get it so much anymore, but you couldn't tell that time we had anything around here, I know it was made out of Poma, you know, had kind of stuff like that to drink, you know, but you'd get this Bhatki, and we call it a big Bertha jar, that would be a 64 ounces of Bhatki, that was strong too, so we'd take and put this here, mix it with this Vishnik, see, to get a color in it, about half and half, so you'd drink it, I think that stuff was powerful, Bhatki, that's the last Bhatki ever drink was out in China, you'd walk right through a wall, we'd get drunk on that stuff, and that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many guys that got arrested here for moonshining?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh yeah, I served with a guy in the service one time out in China, getting 30 days up here in the jail, he was bootlegging, he was running whiskey in, took out his car, and everything, he was a Pomeroy at the time, yeah, old Charlie Summerfield, you know, was a sheriff, he used to drink the damn stuff up here, he died of cancer, no he didn't die, he died by himself, it was like a gunshot, he killed himself, you know, Charlie Summerfield, he was widowing once in his life, and I don't know the summer, so I'll explain it, there was a couple of girls in that family, But Summerfield, I heard he was pretty hard on moonshiners. Well, he'd get them, but then he'd drink the booze up too.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Yeah, he'd get picked up quite a few, but the guy lived down in Minnville, they say, had a mortgage on his flier, and he'd, I heard someone say he was from Tennessee, but I don't know, but he made moonshine, and he had a deal with somebody in Seattle, and they brought him the jars, the jug, the gallon jug, but anyway, he never sold anything around here, but they'd come in and take his load out over in the coast, he made awful good stuff, they said, but he never got in there around here, but he paid the mortgage off, you know, he didn't sell it around here because he didn't want to get caught, that was the old story, that was just an old story, a story that he told about it, possibly sold to him. It sounds smart, it sounds smart to me. Yeah, he'd leave other bootleggers around, he'd be like, I know, you go buy a pint from a guy, buy a pint of whiskey, at that time it was, oh, generally it was three dollars for a pint, but you got a full pint, you know, $4 .69. So the guy said, well, I'll tell you where it's at in a little while. So then he'd come back, he'd come in the pool, and I said, you go down a certain street, and there's some grass down there around a certain telephone pole, a certain place, and you reach down there and you'll find a pint of whiskey, that's how you pick it up.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: He didn't give it to you, but I think he'd take your money and tell you where to go get it, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: The guy stayed in business a while, he did something smart like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think most of the stuff that was drunk around here was bootlegged in here, or do you think it was made in here?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: It wasn't made too damn far from here, at those days it was too far away. When I came back from California one time, I bought a King 8 Roaster car, it was painted red.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I was in the service, I had a little racket going, I was working in the Cali, if I needed anything I could always throw a sack of sugar in the car. That thing didn't have to have much money. But anyway, I got here and I was going on over to Seattle, and this fellow named Babcock wanted to buy it, so he's dead now. He wanted to buy this car for him. He sold it to me for $150 for him. Well, they only had part of the money, so he said I'll send you the money. Well, I only had it three or four months before going over to Seattle to stay three or four months. So he runs them on, he drives them to Canada to bring them back. He drives them, I took that car back when they came back and had some bullet holes in it, I guess it was a bit of a shake. But of course that time that V8 was traveling 90 miles an hour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd probably outrun any police car?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: That could have been what it was, the V8, King 8 was a damn good car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I had a Colgate touring car one time too, and I had a Saxon, you know what?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: And a Maxwell car, that's Benny used to talk about years ago, they had those here. Star car, Graham car, had a Moon car, Liberty car, and of course the big companies tuck them in. Like General Motors took in a lot of them, and had a Chevrolet all right. They had lots of kind of cars, but I got a Chandler car, and Chandler, Chalmers car. Those were the names of the companies that started up and sold some cars, but they were either bought up or went broke, something like that. A little car, had a Star, four cylinder car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So funny, you haven't had a Whippet?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Here in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you drive very far when you had the first cars?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Well the Whippet car drove down to, my brother was in the service down in Vancouver, Washington. He came up, took him back down and drove back again. He couldn't make only about 30 -35 miles an hour. It took a day and a half to drive the trip back out. Nowadays I can drive down to Portland in 6 hours, 5 -6 hours. Of course the roads are not different, you got a lot of things there. That little car I got out here has been to California 9 times. Well I've taken it down every year up to 272 the last year I was down it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you heard about them?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: No, not much of anything about it. I know World War I and so on, I had a fence down there where the West Park School is. That place down there was a fair rung, but it was a tractor. They had some to put in there, some guys that said IWW. But every once in a while, those days we had trash machines here and everything was shocked. Somebody was headed, you know. IWW sticks these wooden messages in the hand to go through the cellars on the tractor machine and it started to fire. But mostly it was smut that caused it. I don't think they were doing any damage. Of course those days, they would hire these guys to drive vinyl teams or pitch windows and feel a salt sack and so on. They were all transient people. They made a call, a winter stake, you know, doing that kind of work. About one time I was younger, so I'm working over there, knocking apples at Wenatchee. Then throw another ketchup at our school bus, you know. And so we went down to the yard and had a freight train thing. I had one change on it, remember. I could cross over the bison and everyone places hung up for a little while, probably wasn't going to stop and eat. Anyway, some guys around there come by. You boys gotta ride this train, you gotta join up. Five dollar a piece, going IWW. Well, we got on the truck. I said, we ain't got any money, so I guess we ain't gonna join. So you gotta get off the train. Well, I guess we'll have to get off the train, I said.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: So we got off the train, see. It pulled out, then again, they were empty box, they're going in the saddle, they swung on again, see. And I pulled the door, but we didn't pull it shut, so nobody could lock it or stop the game. As long as the guy couldn't swing over and get in, see, because he couldn't get down to open the door, nobody could walk over the top. So then we rode it in, the piano thing, and got out. That was the only time I ever had anybody know something about throwing that long IWW thing. We had some money, but you know, we didn't want to spend it on that. Riding the rod we were, you know, by that guy. You get three or four guys pretty tough, you know, they think you got it, you pay up, so you ain't got it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there many guys riding the rails like that?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh, a lot of those guys, yeah. I never played in the fair, green hues, Spokane hardly ever. I rode the blind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where the blinds are at?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: No.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Get right in behind the coal car, the baggage car, the place in there to stand, but you gotta stand in there for a short distance, you know? Where did you pick it up? Here in Moscow?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Oh, yeah, I was going to go to Spokane. Might want to come out of Spokane, I might take a ticket out as far as Marshall. Get off, and before I started, you can jump on that way, right the red way into Moscow. Yeah, you could. Oh, the people rode them that way.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: What about the Dicks? Oh, yeah, in the easier terminal, you'd have the railroad Dicks in the terminal, you'd come down there and hang around, and ride around the aisles, you know?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Like when Nancy in Spokane, you know, ... had a big terminal, and when Nancy had a big terminal, you know, Seattle did like that. So, those things could happen, you know, like that.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Any other little town there, branch line, good, you know?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I guess that railroads, a lot of these railroad men on trains, they didn't care if the guys ran. Oh, no, no, they didn't care. They ain't going to get out of the caboose, they're riding a freight train anyway, they can help it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like working in the orchards over there in Wenatchee? Was there a lot of families over there picking too?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Mostly transits, there's families, yeah, sure there's families, but you know, I paid, I think it was three dollars a week for a bed on a porch. I had a canvas around it, you know, sleep there, see, of course, it was summer months, you know, and fall, oh, yeah, it was October.

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: You got five cents a box for picking apples, and you'd pick like hell for ten hours, but I got for an average stunt like me to pick maybe a hundred, a hundred and twenty bucks an apple, see? Five cents a box, that's five to six dollars a month, so you're making a day, see?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: And you had to pay, you had to buy food, you know, I'd eaten a restaurant, and have lunch, packed up for me, and so on, of course, you know, meals those days wasn't too awful expensive, I had to buy a good meal for fifty cents, sometimes thirty, thirty -five, you know. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you make good money then when the work was done?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: Did you have a lot, or was it... Oh, it saved a little money, you spent a lot too, for example. I had a little trade, and I used to run around the country, the kids were everywhere, went into the service. Those days, I didn't have washing machines, and all these rest, you'd stop into the restroom, you'd need a dishwasher, and damn, they're all of them needed a dishwasher. You'd get your something to eat, value gun, get paid a couple of dollars, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would you stay in a place?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
words: I'd say, I'd stay at a job for twelve, twelve hours, I'd want to move on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You did that around a lot in this country?

speaker: Kenneth Steffen
